panel.origin: An interval is the distance between two pitches. The unison is the same note; the octave is the next note of the same name, eight scale steps up.
panel.chemistry: An octave doubles the frequency — play A at 440 Hz and the A above is 880 Hz. Our ears hear that 2:1 ratio as 'the same note, higher,' which is why both are called A.
panel.mixology: Octaves thicken a melody and add power without changing the harmony. Doubling a bass line an octave up is one of the oldest tricks for fullness.
panel.examples: Somewhere Over the Rainbow opens with an octave leap on 'Some-where' — the single most recognizable octave in music.

panel.origin: A third spans three scale steps. The major third is bright and happy; the minor third is darker and sadder. This one interval decides a chord's whole mood.
panel.chemistry: A major third is four half-steps; a minor third is three. That single half-step difference is the line between a major and a minor chord — the emotional heart of Western harmony.
panel.mixology: Stack thirds to build every basic chord. Want a sadder version of any major chord? Lower its third by a half-step.
panel.examples: The major third opens 'Oh When the Saints'; the minor third is the 'Greensleeves' / 'Stairway' melancholy color.

panel.origin: The interval spanning five scale steps — the most stable and consonant interval after the octave, and the backbone of the circle of fifths.
panel.chemistry: Its frequencies sit in a clean 3:2 ratio, which the ear hears as open and strong. It's so stable it sounds almost hollow on its own.
panel.mixology: Fifths anchor harmony and define a key's home (tonic) and away (dominant). Rock power chords are nothing but stacked fifths.
panel.examples: The Star Wars main theme leaps a perfect fifth; 'Twinkle, Twinkle' jumps one between its first two notes.

panel.origin: The interval of four scale steps — strong and consonant, the inversion of the perfect fifth. Stable but with a slight pull to resolve.
panel.chemistry: A 4:3 frequency ratio. Flip a perfect fifth upside down and you get a perfect fourth; the two are mirror images within the octave.
panel.mixology: Fourths give a bold, open, sometimes heroic sound. Quartal harmony (chords built in fourths) is a staple of modern jazz.
panel.examples: 'Here Comes the Bride' and the opening of 'Amazing Grace' both rise a perfect fourth.

panel.origin: The interval splitting the octave exactly in half — three whole steps. The most dissonant, unstable interval, historically nicknamed 'the devil in music.'
panel.chemistry: It sits at the awkward midpoint with no simple frequency ratio, so the ear hears maximum tension. That tension is exactly what makes it useful.
panel.mixology: The tritone inside a dominant-seventh chord is what creates the urge to resolve home. Tension you can deploy on purpose.
panel.examples: 'Maria' from West Side Story opens on a tritone; it's also the wailing two-note siren of The Simpsons theme.
panel.flag: Medieval theorists called it 'diabolus in musica' — the devil in music — for its unsettling sound.

panel.origin: Larger intervals: the sixth is sweet and lyrical; the seventh is tense and yearning, just shy of the octave.
panel.chemistry: Sixths are mild consonances that sound rich and warm. Sevenths are dissonances that strongly want to step up to the octave, creating forward motion.
panel.mixology: Sixths make lovely harmony lines for two singers or guitars. Sevenths add the color and tension that define jazz chords.
panel.examples: 'My Bonnie Lies Over the Ocean' opens on a sixth; the major-seventh stretch colors countless jazz ballads.

panel.origin: Consonance is the quality of intervals that sound stable and at rest; dissonance is tension that wants to resolve. The push and pull between them drives all music.
panel.chemistry: Simple frequency ratios (octave 2:1, fifth 3:2) sound consonant; complex ones (tritone, seconds, sevenths) sound dissonant. But it's also cultural — what counts as 'harsh' has shifted over centuries.
panel.mixology: Music breathes by moving from tension to release. Set up dissonance, then resolve it to consonance — that arc is the basic engine of emotional effect.
panel.examples: Every suspension in a hymn, every bluesy clash, every jazz chord that 'wants' to move is consonance and dissonance at work.

panel.origin: The seven-note scale that sounds bright and 'complete' — do-re-mi-fa-sol-la-ti-do. The reference scale for nearly all Western music.
panel.chemistry: Its pattern of whole and half steps is fixed: W-W-H-W-W-W-H. That exact sequence is what makes any major scale sound major, no matter which note you start on.
panel.mixology: It's the foundation everything else is measured against. Learn it in every key and most other theory falls into place.
panel.examples: 'Do-Re-Mi' from The Sound of Music literally teaches it; 'Joy to the World' is a major scale descending.

panel.origin: The basic minor scale — darker and more somber than major. The 'sad' scale, also called the Aeolian mode.
panel.chemistry: It flattens the 3rd, 6th, and 7th degrees of the major scale (pattern W-H-W-W-H-W-W). Those three lowered notes are the source of its melancholy.
panel.mixology: Reach for it whenever you want a sad, serious, or moody feel. It shares all its notes with its relative major.
panel.examples: 'Greensleeves,' 'Scarborough Fair,' and a huge amount of folk and metal live in natural minor.

panel.origin: A minor scale with a raised 7th, giving an exotic, dramatic flavor and a strong pull back to the tonic.
panel.chemistry: Raising the 7th creates a leading tone that resolves strongly home, but it also opens an unusual step-and-a-half gap that gives the scale its Middle-Eastern/Spanish color.
panel.mixology: Use it when minor-key harmony needs a strong dominant chord, or when you want that dramatic, slightly exotic sound.
panel.examples: The 'Sherlock'/spy-movie tension sound and much flamenco and classical minor-key music use harmonic minor.

panel.origin: A minor scale that raises both the 6th and 7th going up, then often reverts to natural minor coming down — built to make minor-key melodies flow smoothly.
panel.chemistry: The raised 6th and 7th smooth out the awkward leap of harmonic minor when a melody is ascending. Composers traditionally lowered them again on the way down.
panel.mixology: A go-to for graceful minor-key melody writing, and (in its ascending form) a core scale in jazz improvisation.
panel.examples: Bach's minor-key melodies and a great deal of jazz soloing draw on melodic minor.

panel.origin: A five-note scale that sounds good over almost anything — the friendliest scale in music, with no 'wrong' notes.
panel.chemistry: It removes the two most tension-prone notes from the major or minor scale, leaving five that consistently sound consonant together. That's why it's nearly foolproof.
panel.mixology: The first scale to learn for improvising. Solo over a blues or pop song with the matching pentatonic and you'll rarely hit a clashing note.
panel.examples: Countless guitar solos, 'Amazing Grace,' 'My Girl,' and folk music worldwide are pentatonic.

panel.origin: The pentatonic minor scale plus one extra note — the 'blue note' — that gives blues, rock, and jazz their gritty, expressive sound.
panel.chemistry: Adding a flatted fifth (the blue note) between the 4th and 5th creates a sliding, dissonant tension that's the signature of the blues.
panel.mixology: The default vocabulary for blues and rock soloing. Bend into and out of the blue note for that crying, vocal quality.
panel.examples: Every blues guitar solo, plus the swagger of countless rock riffs, leans on the blues scale.

panel.origin: Seven scales made by starting the major scale on each of its different notes — the same notes, but a different 'home,' giving each a distinct mood.
panel.chemistry: Start C major on D and you get D Dorian; on E, E Phrygian, and so on. Same seven notes, but the new tonic shifts which intervals fall where, changing the color completely.
panel.mixology: Modes let you stay in one set of notes while conjuring very different moods — Dorian's cool jazz, Phrygian's Spanish edge, Lydian's dreamy lift.
panel.examples: 'So What' (Dorian), Spanish flamenco (Phrygian), and The Simpsons theme (Lydian) showcase the modes.

panel.origin: The scale of all twelve notes — every half-step within the octave, with no gaps.
panel.chemistry: It includes every pitch Western music uses, so it has no key center of its own. It's raw material rather than a 'home' scale.
panel.mixology: Use chromatic runs and passing tones to connect other notes smoothly and add tension, color, or a sense of slithering motion.
panel.examples: 'Flight of the Bumblebee' is almost pure chromatic motion; jazz fills are full of chromatic passing notes.

panel.origin: The basic three-note chord, built by stacking two thirds. Major, minor, diminished, and augmented triads are the building blocks of harmony.
panel.chemistry: Take a root, add the note a third above, then another third on top. The size of those thirds (major or minor) decides the chord's quality and mood.
panel.mixology: Triads are the harmonic vocabulary of most songs. Knowing the four types and how to build them on any note covers an enormous amount of music.
panel.examples: Nearly every pop, folk, and rock song is built on major and minor triads.

panel.origin: A triad with one more third stacked on top, adding a 4th note (the seventh). Richer and more colorful than a plain triad.
panel.chemistry: The added seventh introduces mild tension and sophistication. Dominant sevenths in particular create a strong pull to resolve, driving harmony forward.
panel.mixology: Sevenths are the entry point to jazzier, more emotional harmony. Swap a plain chord for its seventh to instantly add color.
panel.examples: The bluesy dominant sevenths of every blues tune, and the lush major sevenths of jazz and R&B ballads.

panel.origin: The same chord with a note other than the root in the bass — same notes, different bottom, different feel.
panel.chemistry: Move the root up an octave and the third sits in the bass (first inversion); move the third up too and the fifth is in the bass (second inversion). The harmony is unchanged, but the color and smoothness shift.
panel.mixology: Inversions let the bass line move smoothly between chords instead of leaping, and they vary the texture of repeated chords.
panel.examples: Pachelbel's Canon and countless hymns use inversions to create a stepwise, flowing bass.

panel.origin: Chords stacked beyond the seventh — adding the 9th, 11th, or 13th for lush, complex color.
panel.chemistry: Keep stacking thirds above a seventh chord and you reach the 9th, 11th, and 13th. Each added note brings more richness and ambiguity.
panel.mixology: These are the sophisticated colors of jazz, funk, soul, and film scoring. Use them where a plain chord would feel too simple.
panel.examples: The shimmering chords of jazz, the funk of Stevie Wonder, and lush film scores all use extensions.

panel.origin: A chord where the third is replaced by the note just above or below it (the 4th or 2nd), creating an open, unresolved sound that 'hangs.'
panel.chemistry: With no third, a sus chord is neither major nor minor — it sits in suspense, usually wanting to resolve to the regular chord.
panel.mixology: Use sus chords to delay resolution and build gentle tension, then drop to the plain chord for release. Great for intros and atmosphere.
panel.examples: The opening of 'Pinball Wizard' and the jangle of countless folk-rock songs lean on sus chords.

panel.origin: Just the root and the fifth — no third — so it's neither major nor minor. The raw, heavy backbone of rock and metal.
panel.chemistry: Dropping the third removes the major/minor flavor and any harmonic 'sweetness,' leaving a thick, neutral sound that holds up under distortion.
panel.mixology: Play them on a distorted electric guitar for power and aggression. Their neutrality lets them sit over any harmony.
panel.examples: Virtually every rock and metal riff — from 'Smoke on the Water' to punk — is built on power chords.

panel.origin: Two tense, unstable triads: the diminished (two stacked minor thirds) sounds dark and anxious; the augmented (two major thirds) sounds eerie and unresolved.
panel.chemistry: Both break the comfortable major/minor mold. Diminished chords are full of tritone tension; augmented chords stretch the fifth wider until it floats.
panel.mixology: Use them as tension chords that demand resolution, or as colorful passing chords between stable ones. Staples of suspense and surprise.
panel.examples: Horror and suspense scores lean on diminished chords; the dreamy unease of augmented chords colors many film and jazz moments.

panel.origin: The three-chord backbone of Western music — the I, IV, and V chords of a key. With these alone you can play thousands of songs.
panel.chemistry: These are the three major chords built on the 1st, 4th, and 5th degrees of a scale. Together they cover every note of the key, so almost any melody fits over them.
panel.mixology: The first progression to learn. Master I–IV–V in a few keys and you can play along with an enormous swath of folk, rock, country, and pop.
panel.examples: 'Twist and Shout,' 'La Bamba,' 'Wild Thing,' and most early rock 'n' roll are I–IV–V.

panel.origin: The most important progression in jazz — a smooth, satisfying path home through the ii, V, and I chords.
panel.chemistry: The ii leads naturally to the V (a fifth away), which has a strong pull to the I. Each chord sets up the next, creating an irresistible sense of arrival.
panel.mixology: Recognize and practice ii–V–I in every key and you've unlocked the grammar of jazz standards. It's the progression that ties countless tunes together.
panel.examples: 'Autumn Leaves,' 'Fly Me to the Moon,' and most jazz standards are built from chains of ii–V–I.

panel.origin: The strongest way to end a phrase — V moving to I — that sounds fully resolved, like a musical period at the end of a sentence.
panel.chemistry: The dominant (V) chord contains the tension-laden leading tone and tritone that pull hard toward the tonic (I). Landing on I releases all that tension: home at last.
panel.mixology: Use it to end sections and songs with a sense of finality. It's the default 'the end' in tonal music.
panel.examples: The decisive final chords of nearly every classical piece and hymn.

panel.origin: The 'Amen' cadence — IV moving to I — a softer, more peaceful resolution than the authentic cadence.
panel.chemistry: Moving from the subdominant (IV) to the tonic (I) resolves gently, without the sharp leading-tone pull of a V–I. The effect is calm and settled rather than emphatic.
panel.mixology: Use it for a gentle, reverent, or relaxed ending — anywhere a full authentic cadence would feel too forceful.
panel.examples: The 'A-men' sung at the end of hymns is the textbook plagal cadence.

panel.origin: A surprise ending — the V chord sets you up to land on I, but resolves somewhere unexpected (usually vi) instead.
panel.chemistry: The ear expects V to resolve to I. Substituting the vi chord (which shares two notes with I) gives a soft landing that's a surprise without being jarring — resolution deferred.
panel.mixology: Use it to extend a phrase, dodge a too-early ending, or create a moment of wistful surprise before the real cadence arrives.
panel.examples: Composers from Bach to film scorers use deceptive cadences to keep a piece from ending before its time.

panel.origin: A 12-measure chord pattern using I, IV, and V — the form underneath most blues and a huge amount of early rock 'n' roll.
panel.chemistry: It arranges the three primary chords into a fixed 12-bar cycle with a predictable shape, leaving room for endless melodic and lyrical variation over the same frame.
panel.mixology: Learn the 12-bar pattern and you can jam with blues musicians anywhere — it's a shared language. A perfect frame for practicing soloing.
panel.examples: 'Johnny B. Goode,' 'Hound Dog,' and the entire blues tradition run on the 12-bar form.

panel.origin: A chord progression that moves by descending fifths — following the circle of fifths — producing a powerfully natural sense of forward motion.
panel.chemistry: Each chord sits a fifth below the last, so every change feels like the strongest possible resolution, chained one after another. The ear is pulled continuously forward.
panel.mixology: Use a circle progression when you want momentum and inevitability, or to travel smoothly through several chords back to home.
panel.examples: 'Hey Joe,' 'I Will Survive,' and the turnarounds of countless jazz tunes use circle-of-fifths motion.

panel.origin: The beat is the steady pulse you tap your foot to; tempo is how fast that pulse goes, measured in beats per minute (BPM).
panel.chemistry: The beat is the underlying grid of time; tempo sets its speed. A slow 60 BPM is one beat a second; a fast dance track might run 128 BPM or more.
panel.mixology: Lock to the beat to play in time, and choose a tempo that fits the feel — slow for ballads, fast for energy. A metronome trains both.
panel.examples: Every song you can clap along to is built on a beat; the difference between a lullaby and a dance track is largely tempo.

panel.origin: The two stacked numbers at the start of music that tell you how beats are grouped — like 4/4, 3/4, or 6/8.
panel.chemistry: The top number is how many beats per measure; the bottom is which note value gets the beat. 4/4 is four quarter-notes a bar; 3/4 is the three-beat waltz feel.
panel.mixology: Knowing the time signature tells you where the strong beats fall, so you can feel the groove and place accents correctly.
panel.examples: 4/4 powers nearly all pop and rock; 3/4 is every waltz; 6/8 gives the lilt of many ballads and jigs.

panel.origin: The symbols showing how long each note lasts — whole, half, quarter, eighth, sixteenth — each half the length of the one before.
panel.chemistry: It's a halving system: a whole note splits into two halves, a half into two quarters, and so on. Dots and ties extend these basic durations.
panel.mixology: Reading note values lets you play rhythms accurately from a page and count complex passages by subdividing the beat.
panel.examples: The steady quarter-notes of a march versus the rapid sixteenths of a drum fill are note values at work.

panel.origin: Rhythm that accents the off-beats — the 'and' between the main beats — creating a sense of surprise, groove, and momentum.
panel.chemistry: By placing stresses where the ear expects rest (and vice versa), syncopation works against the steady pulse, which is exactly what makes it feel lively and propulsive.
panel.mixology: Use syncopation to make a rhythm groove instead of plod. It's the difference between stiff and funky.
panel.examples: Ragtime, funk, reggae's off-beat skank, and most catchy pop hooks are deeply syncopated.

panel.origin: A rhythmic feel where pairs of notes are played long-short instead of even, giving jazz and blues their characteristic bounce.
panel.chemistry: Instead of dividing the beat into two equal halves, swing stretches the first and shortens the second (roughly a 2:1 lilt), creating a rolling, triplet-based groove.
panel.mixology: Apply swing to straight eighth-notes to instantly turn a stiff line jazzy. It's felt more than counted — you learn it by listening.
panel.examples: Big-band jazz, blues shuffles, and 'It Don't Mean a Thing (If It Ain't Got That Swing)' define the feel.

panel.origin: Two conflicting rhythms played at once — like three beats against two — creating rich, complex, often hypnotic textures.
panel.chemistry: Each rhythm keeps its own pulse, and the ear hears both grids at once. Where they line up and pull apart creates the characteristic tension and motion.
panel.mixology: Use polyrhythm to add depth and sophistication, layering a contrasting rhythm over the main groove. Central to African and much modern music.
panel.examples: West African drumming, much of Steve Reich's music, and progressive rock and metal thrive on polyrhythm.

panel.origin: The numbered positions within a scale (1 through 7), each with a name and a characteristic 'pull' — like the restful tonic or the restless leading tone.
panel.chemistry: Every note of a key has a role: the 1st (tonic) is home, the 5th (dominant) is the main tension, the 7th (leading tone) strains upward to resolve. These roles, not the letter names, drive harmony.
panel.mixology: Thinking in scale degrees (or Roman numerals) lets you understand and transpose music in any key — the relationships stay the same even when the notes change.
panel.examples: Solfège ('do-re-mi') is scale degrees by another name; it's how singers internalize a key.

panel.origin: The two harmonic poles of a key: the tonic (I) is home and rest; the dominant (V) is tension that pulls back to it. The basic gravity of tonal music.
panel.chemistry: The dominant chord contains the leading tone and a tritone, both straining toward the tonic. That built-in tension-and-release is the engine of nearly all Western harmony.
panel.mixology: Set up the dominant, then resolve to the tonic, to create satisfying arrival. Almost every phrase and cadence relies on this relationship.
panel.examples: The 'away then home' feeling at the end of virtually every classical phrase and pop chorus.

panel.origin: The chords built using only the notes of one key — the seven chords that 'belong' together and form the harmonic palette of most songs.
panel.chemistry: Stack thirds on each scale degree and you get a fixed set of chord qualities (in major: I, ii, iii, IV, V, vi, vii°). These are the chords that naturally fit a key.
panel.mixology: Knowing a key's diatonic chords tells you which chords will sound 'right' together, so you can build progressions and harmonize melodies confidently.
panel.examples: Most pop songs use only diatonic chords; the famous I–V–vi–IV pop progression is pure diatonic harmony.

panel.origin: Changing from one key to another within a piece — shifting the harmonic home to create contrast, lift, or drama.
panel.chemistry: A modulation usually pivots through a chord shared by both keys, then confirms the new home with its own dominant. Done smoothly, the ear is led somewhere new without feeling lost.
panel.mixology: Use modulation for a fresh-energy key change in a final chorus, or to give a longer piece structural variety and travel.
panel.examples: The triumphant final-chorus key-bump in countless pop songs, and the elaborate modulations of classical sonatas.

panel.origin: The art of moving each individual melodic line (voice) smoothly from chord to chord, usually by small steps rather than leaps.
panel.chemistry: Good voice-leading keeps common tones held, moves other voices to the nearest available note, and avoids awkward parallels. The chords connect like interlocking gears.
panel.mixology: Apply it to make chord progressions sound smooth and professional, and to write inner harmony parts that are satisfying to sing or play.
panel.examples: The seamless part-writing of Bach chorales is the gold standard of voice-leading.

panel.origin: A chord borrowed from outside the key that acts as the dominant of a chord other than the tonic — a temporary detour that adds color and pull.
panel.chemistry: It's a 'V of' something: a dominant chord aimed at a chord that isn't the key's home, momentarily treating that chord as a temporary tonic before resolving to it.
panel.mixology: Use secondary dominants to add richness and forward drive to an otherwise plain diatonic progression — a splash of harmonic spice.
panel.examples: The surprise bright chord that 'leads somewhere' in jazz standards and sophisticated pop is often a secondary dominant.

panel.origin: The five-line staff where notes are written, and the clefs (treble, bass) that fix which pitches the lines and spaces represent.
panel.chemistry: A note's vertical position on the staff shows its pitch; the clef at the start anchors the whole system — treble for higher instruments and voices, bass for lower.
panel.mixology: Reading the staff and clefs is the entry point to sheet music. The mnemonics ('Every Good Boy…', 'FACE') get you started fast.
panel.examples: Any piece of sheet music you've ever seen relies on the staff and a clef.

panel.origin: The group of sharps or flats at the start of each line that tells you which key the music is in, so you don't mark every accidental individually.
panel.chemistry: The number and pattern of sharps or flats maps directly to a key (1 sharp = G major, 2 flats = B♭ major, and so on). It's shorthand drawn straight from the circle of fifths.
panel.mixology: Read the key signature first to know which notes are consistently sharp or flat, and to identify the key (and its likely chords) at a glance.
panel.examples: Every printed score opens with a key signature; recognizing them is half of sight-reading.

panel.origin: The markings that tell you how loud or soft to play — from pianissimo (very soft) to fortissimo (very loud) — and how to shade between them.
panel.chemistry: Italian terms and symbols set relative volume (p, f) and gradual change (crescendo to grow, decrescendo to fade). They shape the emotional contour of a performance.
panel.mixology: Use dynamics to give music life and direction — swelling into a climax, pulling back for intimacy. Flat dynamics make even great notes feel lifeless.
panel.examples: The hushed-to-thunderous swells of a film score or symphony are dynamics doing the emotional heavy lifting.

panel.origin: The most common song form in popular music: alternating verses (same music, new words) with a repeating chorus (the memorable hook).
panel.chemistry: Verses advance the story while the chorus delivers the central, repeated emotional payoff. A bridge often adds contrast before a final chorus. The structure balances novelty and familiarity.
panel.mixology: Use it as the default blueprint for writing a catchy song: verse, chorus, verse, chorus, bridge, chorus.
panel.examples: The overwhelming majority of pop, rock, and country hits follow verse–chorus form.

panel.origin: A 32-bar song form in four 8-bar sections: a main melody (A) repeated, a contrasting bridge (B), then the main melody again. The backbone of the Great American Songbook.
panel.chemistry: Three statements of the A section frame a single contrasting B section (the 'middle eight'). The repetition makes the tune memorable; the bridge keeps it from growing stale.
panel.mixology: Reach for AABA when writing in a classic, standards-style idiom, or to give a melody a satisfying, balanced shape.
panel.examples: 'Somewhere Over the Rainbow,' 'I Got Rhythm,' and most jazz standards are AABA.

panel.origin: A large-scale classical form in three parts — exposition, development, recapitulation — that presents themes, explores them, then brings them home.
panel.chemistry: The exposition states two contrasting themes in different keys; the development fragments and transforms them through distant keys; the recapitulation returns them, now reconciled in the home key.
panel.mixology: Understanding sonata form is the key to following the architecture of most classical first movements — knowing where you are in the journey.
panel.examples: The opening movements of Mozart, Haydn, and Beethoven symphonies and sonatas are built on sonata form.

INTERVALS.en: Intervals are pure ratios: the octave is exactly 2:1 and the perfect fifth is 3:2. Pythagoras is said to have discovered these whole-number relationships over 2,500 years ago.
INTERVALS.es: Intervals are pure ratios: the octave is exactly 2:1 and the perfect fifth is 3:2. Pythagoras is said to have discovered these whole-number relationships over 2,500 years ago.
INTERVALS.fr: Intervals are pure ratios: the octave is exactly 2:1 and the perfect fifth is 3:2. Pythagoras is said to have discovered these whole-number relationships over 2,500 years ago.
INTERVALS.it: Intervals are pure ratios: the octave is exactly 2:1 and the perfect fifth is 3:2. Pythagoras is said to have discovered these whole-number relationships over 2,500 years ago.

SCALES & MODES.en: The modes have ancient Greek names — Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian — but the medieval church mixed up which scale went with which name, so the labels we use today don't match the original Greek scales.
SCALES & MODES.es: The modes have ancient Greek names — Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian — but the medieval church mixed up which scale went with which name, so the labels we use today don't match the original Greek scales.
SCALES & MODES.fr: The modes have ancient Greek names — Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian — but the medieval church mixed up which scale went with which name, so the labels we use today don't match the original Greek scales.
SCALES & MODES.it: The modes have ancient Greek names — Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian — but the medieval church mixed up which scale went with which name, so the labels we use today don't match the original Greek scales.

CHORDS.en: Western harmony is built almost entirely by stacking thirds. Start on any note, skip every other scale note, and you've built the chord — triads, sevenths, and jazz extensions all come from that one trick.
CHORDS.es: Western harmony is built almost entirely by stacking thirds. Start on any note, skip every other scale note, and you've built the chord — triads, sevenths, and jazz extensions all come from that one trick.
CHORDS.fr: Western harmony is built almost entirely by stacking thirds. Start on any note, skip every other scale note, and you've built the chord — triads, sevenths, and jazz extensions all come from that one trick.
CHORDS.it: Western harmony is built almost entirely by stacking thirds. Start on any note, skip every other scale note, and you've built the chord — triads, sevenths, and jazz extensions all come from that one trick.

PROGRESSIONS & CADENCES.en: Four chords — I, V, vi, and IV — power a startling number of pop hits. The comedy group Axis of Awesome famously medleyed dozens of songs over that single progression.
PROGRESSIONS & CADENCES.es: Four chords — I, V, vi, and IV — power a startling number of pop hits. The comedy group Axis of Awesome famously medleyed dozens of songs over that single progression.
PROGRESSIONS & CADENCES.fr: Four chords — I, V, vi, and IV — power a startling number of pop hits. The comedy group Axis of Awesome famously medleyed dozens of songs over that single progression.
PROGRESSIONS & CADENCES.it: Four chords — I, V, vi, and IV — power a startling number of pop hits. The comedy group Axis of Awesome famously medleyed dozens of songs over that single progression.

RHYTHM & METER.en: Most of the Western world moves in 4/4, but plenty of beloved music doesn't: waltzes are in 3, and songs like 'Take Five' (5/4) and 'Money' (7/4) prove odd meters can still be hits.
RHYTHM & METER.es: Most of the Western world moves in 4/4, but plenty of beloved music doesn't: waltzes are in 3, and songs like 'Take Five' (5/4) and 'Money' (7/4) prove odd meters can still be hits.
RHYTHM & METER.fr: Most of the Western world moves in 4/4, but plenty of beloved music doesn't: waltzes are in 3, and songs like 'Take Five' (5/4) and 'Money' (7/4) prove odd meters can still be hits.
RHYTHM & METER.it: Most of the Western world moves in 4/4, but plenty of beloved music doesn't: waltzes are in 3, and songs like 'Take Five' (5/4) and 'Money' (7/4) prove odd meters can still be hits.

HARMONY & FUNCTION.en: The pull of the dominant chord back to the tonic comes from a tritone hidden inside it — the same 'devil's interval' medieval monks tried to avoid is the engine that drives almost all our music home.
HARMONY & FUNCTION.es: The pull of the dominant chord back to the tonic comes from a tritone hidden inside it — the same 'devil's interval' medieval monks tried to avoid is the engine that drives almost all our music home.

HARMONY & FUNCTION.it: The pull of the dominant chord back to the tonic comes from a tritone hidden inside it — the same 'devil's interval' medieval monks tried to avoid is the engine that drives almost all our music home.

FORM & NOTATION.en: Our five-line staff is roughly a thousand years old, credited to the monk Guido of Arezzo, who also invented the do-re-mi singing system still taught today.
